parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker supplies `dir` -> it bypasses any whitelist -> f-string builds `q` with `direction` -> `db.fetch_all(q)` executes attacker-influenced SQL.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers may manipulate query execution (and possibly read or corrupt data depending on DB/driver settings).
parsed_findings.proof: Example: `?sort=name&dir=asc;--` yields `... ORDER BY name asc;--` executed by `db.fetch_all(q)`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a request with `sort` set to an allowed value (e.g., `name`) and `dir` set to `asc;--` (or another `ORDER BY` payload) and observe altered query behavior.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `dir` is interpolated into an SQL `ORDER BY` clause without validation, enabling SQL injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker supplies internal target URL in `req.query.url` -> server-side `axios.get` performs the request -> `res.send(data.data)` returns the fetched content to the attacker.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can access internal services (and possibly cloud metadata) and exfiltrate sensitive responses through the endpoint.
parsed_findings.proof: Request `/fetch?url=http://127.0.0.1:2375/containers/json` would cause the server to query the internal Docker daemon and return its response body.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: 1) Start the app and note it serves `/fetch` 2) Send `GET /fetch?url=http://127.0.0.1:80/` (or other internal host) 3) Observe the response contains the internal host content.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The route fetches an attacker-controlled `url` via `axios.get` and returns the result, enabling SSRF to internal network resources.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Invalid inv_id -> inv=None -> crash at inv.project_id -> observable error response (DoS/ID enumeration).
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can cause 500 errors (DoS) and potentially distinguish valid vs invalid IDs based on response behavior.
parsed_findings.proof: When inv_id does not exist, inv is None and inv.project_id raises an exception at the authorization check.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a PATCH /api/invoices/<inv_id> request with a non-existent inv_id and any JSON body (e.g., {"note":"x"}).
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: update_invoice does not handle db.get_invoice(inv_id) returning None before accessing inv.project_id, which can trigger a server error for invalid invoice IDs.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Traverse out of `/srv/data` via repeated `../` segments, then `fs.readFile` returns the targeted file contents as `text/plain`.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can exfiltrate arbitrary server files readable by the Node process, potentially exposing secrets and escalating follow-on attacks.
parsed_findings.proof: Supplying `file=../../secret.txt` leaves `../secret.txt`, and `path.join('/srv/data',f)` resolves outside the intended directory before `fs.readFile` reads it.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `GET /download?file=../../secret.txt` and observe the response body containing `/srv/secret.txt` (or another traversed target).
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `req.query.file` is only sanitized with a single `replace('../','')`, allowing path traversal and arbitrary file reads under/above `/srv/data`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker crafts malicious pickle -> base64 encodes it -> sets cookie `state` -> victim hits `/resume` -> server runs `pickle.loads` during deserialization.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary code on the server, leading to full account takeover, data theft, or service compromise.
parsed_findings.proof: `pickle.loads(base64.b64decode(raw))` deserializes attacker-controlled `request.cookies['state']` without validation or a safe deserializer.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Set cookie `state` to a base64-encoded malicious pickle that triggers code execution, then request `GET /resume` to deserialize it.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted user-controlled cookie is base64-decoded and passed to `pickle.loads`, enabling remote code execution via malicious pickle payloads.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the updated privileged flags/roles to access endpoints or actions requiring isSupport/admin checks.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can self-escalate privileges or modify protected attributes (e.g., roles/support flags) and gain access to gated functionality.
parsed_findings.proof: Object.assign(user, req.body) copies attacker-controlled properties onto the persisted user object before db.users.save(user).
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Issue PATCH /api/users/<yourId> with body like {"isSupport":true,"role":"admin"} and observe the stored changes.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Mass-assignment allows updating sensitive user fields from req.body without field-level allowlisting.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Inject shell metacharacters into `name`/`src` so the constructed `cmd` terminates `tar` and runs attacker commands when passed to `os.system`.
parsed_findings.impact: Attacker can run arbitrary OS commands on the server, potentially leading to data theft, persistence, or full takeover.
parsed_findings.proof: `cmd = f'tar -czf /tmp/{name}.tgz {src}'` is built from `request.json` values and executed by `os.system(cmd)`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST to `/backup` with JSON `{"name":"x;id #","src":"/var/data/a;id #"}` and observe injected command execution.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `name` and `src` are interpolated into a shell command and executed via `os.system`, enabling command injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: POST -> markdown-> html untrusted -> db.insert_comment stores html -> /comments returns items -> client renders html -> script executes
parsed_findings.impact: Stored XSS can execute attacker JavaScript for anyone who views comments.
parsed_findings.proof: Use `body` like ``; if the Markdown renderer allows raw HTML, it is stored as `html`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/comments` with `{"body":""}` then GET `/comments` and view the comment HTML in a browser context.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-supplied `body` is converted to HTML via `markdown.markdown()` and stored without sanitization, enabling stored XSS when rendered in clients.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Pick victim id as `to` 2) Use negative `amount` 3) Submit transfer 4) Observe sender increases and victim decreases
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can steal funds from arbitrary recipients by submitting negative amounts, directly corrupting balances.
parsed_findings.proof: Because `amt` can be negative (line 4) and the check is `if src < amt` (line 6), negative `amt` won’t trigger the error path, then `src-amt` increases sender and `+amt` decreases recipient (lines 8-9).
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send POST `/transfer` with JSON `{"to":"victim","amount":-50}`; the handler will add 50 to your balance and subtract 50 from `to`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `amount` is not validated for sign, so a negative value passes the insufficient-funds check and reverses balance effects.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Start two parallel `/transfer` requests 2) Ensure they overlap around reading `src` 3) Confirm balances diverge from expected serial execution
parsed_findings.impact: Concurrent requests can produce incorrect balances (e.g., double-spend or lost updates), violating financial integrity.
parsed_findings.proof: The code reads `src` (line 5) and then writes sender and recipient balances in two separate calls (lines 8-9) without a transaction/lock.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Run two concurrent transfers from the same account exceeding/interleaving the `src` read; compare results across runs to observe double-spend or lost updates.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Balance updates are not performed atomically, enabling inconsistent state under concurrent transfers.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1 Trigger jwt.verify failure; 2 Reach production-only jwt.decode fallback; 3 Caller treats returned object as authenticated; 4 Use claims to access protected resources.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can impersonate users and bypass authorization checks by supplying tampered JWTs with arbitrary claims/identity data.
parsed_findings.proof: With NODE_ENV='production' and an invalid-signature JWT, jwt.verify throws and the catch returns jwt.decode(t), yielding attacker-controlled claims.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Set NODE_ENV=production; send a request with Authorization: Bearer <forged JWT with bad signature>; observe verifyToken returns decoded payload instead of rejecting.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: In production, verification errors fall back to jwt.decode(t), which performs no signature/claim validation, enabling authentication bypass with forged tokens.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Parse `id` to integer; evaluate `if uid` truthiness; show falsy `uid=0` skips the forbid branch; confirm DB query still runs on line 7.
parsed_findings.impact: If a user with `id=0` exists, an authenticated attacker may retrieve that record despite the intended restriction; otherwise it’s still an authorization logic flaw.
parsed_findings.proof: Because `int(request.args['id'])` makes `uid=0` falsy, line 5 does not trigger the `return {'error':'forbidden'},403` and line 7 executes the DB query.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Login, then request `GET /u?id=0` and observe it reaches `db.fetch_one(...)` instead of returning 403.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The authorization check uses `if uid:` so nonzero `id` is forbidden, but `id=0` bypasses the 403 and proceeds to the user lookup.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Exploit timing differences to guess bytes/hex characters of the HMAC, then submit a fully correct `X-Sig` to reach `process_event(json.loads(body))`.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker could potentially recover the correct signature via timing analysis, enabling unauthorized webhook processing.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 6 compares HMAC hex digests with `==` instead of a constant-time comparator.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send many `/hook` requests with the same body but varying the provided `X-Sig` near correct values and measure server response latency to correlate partial matches.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The signature check uses `got == want`, which is not constant-time and may leak information via response timing differences.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker crafts `next` with scheme-relative form (`//host`) then triggers `/go` to redirect victims off-site.
parsed_findings.impact: Enables phishing and bypasses trust checks in downstream flows that rely on redirect targets being internal.
parsed_findings.proof: `next='//evil.example'` passes `startswith('/')` and `redirect(nxt)` can produce a Location header pointing to an attacker-controlled host.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send GET /go?next=//evil.example and observe the response Location header redirecting to the external domain.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Open redirect due to insufficient validation of the user-controlled `next` parameter allowing scheme-relative external redirects.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: resolve_entities=True allows external entity expansion, fromstring() processes attacker-controlled XML, and the expanded entity content can trigger outbound requests or data disclosure.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can exfiltrate server files or cause SSRF/OOB requests via external entity resolution.
parsed_findings.proof: Using a DOCTYPE external entity like <!DOCTYPE x [<!ENTITY e SYSTEM "http://attacker/oob">]><root>&e;</root> triggers entity resolution.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST /xml/import with Content-Type: application/xml and body: <!DOCTYPE x [<!ENTITY e SYSTEM "http://attacker/oob">]><root>&e;</root>.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The endpoint enables XXE by using XMLParser(resolve_entities=True) and parsing untrusted request.data with etree.fromstring.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Client sends internal URL -> server makes outbound request -> response reveals fetched content characteristics.
parsed_findings.impact: Attacker can access internal network resources and potentially extract sensitive data via response metadata (e.g., size).
parsed_findings.proof: `url = request.json['url']` then `requests.get(url, timeout=2).content` without allowlist/host validation.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/avatar/fetch` with `{"url":"http://127.0.0.1:80/"}` (or cloud metadata like `http://169.254.169.254/`).
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `url` is fetched server-side with only a `startswith('http')` check, enabling SSRF to internal services.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker hosts huge response -> server loads all bytes into RAM -> process memory pressure leads to DoS.
parsed_findings.impact: Resource exhaustion can degrade or crash the service when responses are large.
parsed_findings.proof: `requests.get(...).content` loads entire response before returning `{'size':len(data)}`; no size cap/streaming.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Run an HTTP endpoint returning a very large body, then POST `/avatar/fetch` with that URL and observe resource exhaustion.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Server downloads full response body into memory via `.content`, allowing memory/CPU DoS with large payloads.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Overflow the return address/nearby stack data via oversized input to hijack control flow when `main` returns.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can corrupt control data and potentially achieve code execution or denial of service.
parsed_findings.proof: `gets(buf)` has no length limit while `buf` is declared as `char buf[64];`, so long input overwrites the stack.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Compile and run, then pipe a payload like 200+ 'A's as input to trigger a crash/overwrite past `buf`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Use of `gets(buf)` reads unbounded input into a 64-byte stack buffer, enabling stack overflow.

results.raw_output: {"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-120","file":"vuln.c","line_start":3,"line_end":6,"vuln_claim":"Use of `gets(buf)` reads unbounded input into a 64-byte stack buffer, enabling stack overflow.","proof":"`gets(buf)` has no length limit while `buf` is declared as `char buf[64];`, so long input overwrites the stack.","repro_steps":"Compile and run, then pipe a payload like 200+ 'A's as input to trigger a crash/overwrite past `buf`.","impact":"An attacker can corrupt control data and potentially achieve code execution or denial of service.","confidence":0.95,"chain_steps":"Overflow the return address/nearby stack data via oversized input to hijack control flow when `main` returns."}]}

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Read app SharedPreferences -> retrieve `auth/access_token` -> use token in Authorization header -> gain unauthorized access.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can steal the access token and impersonate the user to call authenticated APIs.
parsed_findings.proof: `putString('access_token', token)` writes the token directly to SharedPreferences with MODE_PRIVATE but no protection.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Extract app data (e.g., via rooted device, ADB backup, or malware) and read `auth` SharedPreferences for `access_token`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Sensitive auth token is persisted in plain SharedPreferences without encryption or secure storage.

results.raw_output: {"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-312","file":"SessionStore.kt","line_start":2,"line_end":5,"vuln_claim":"Sensitive auth token is persisted in plain SharedPreferences without encryption or secure storage.","proof":"`putString('access_token', token)` writes the token directly to SharedPreferences with MODE_PRIVATE but no protection.","repro_steps":"Extract app data (e.g., via rooted device, ADB backup, or malware) and read `auth` SharedPreferences for `access_token`.","impact":"An attacker can steal the access token and impersonate the user to call authenticated APIs.","confidence":0.78,"chain_steps":"Read app SharedPreferences -> retrieve `auth/access_token` -> use token in Authorization header -> gain unauthorized access."}]}

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Get KEY from code -> decrypt captured iv|ciphertext -> recover plaintexts.
parsed_findings.impact: Loss of confidentiality for all data encrypted with this function across environments.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 2 defines KEY = b'0123456789abcdef' directly in source code, so the secret is not secret.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Read crypto_util.py to obtain KEY, then use it to AES-CBC-decrypt any captured iv+ciphertext.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The AES key is hardcoded as a constant byte string, enabling anyone with code access to decrypt ciphertexts.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Tamper ciphertext blocks/IV -> trigger decrypt -> observe validity/error side effects -> derive or control plaintext.
parsed_findings.impact: Attacker may modify or recover plaintext under chosen-ciphertext conditions, depending on downstream error handling.
parsed_findings.proof: The function returns iv + AES-CBC ciphertext with no MAC/AEAD tag, so tampering is undetectable at decryption time.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Intercept iv+ciphertext -> flip bits in the IV/ciphertext -> send to a decrypting endpoint and use error/behavior to infer plaintext bytes.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: AES-CBC encryption is provided without integrity/authentication, making the ciphertext malleable and potentially vulnerable to padding-oracle style attacks.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: User supplies `out=NULL` with `out_sz>0` -> guard fails -> `memcpy`/`out[n]` dereference NULL -> process fault.
parsed_findings.impact: Crash (denial of service) and potential fault handling bypass if this function is reachable with attacker-controlled pointers.
parsed_findings.proof: When `out==NULL` and `out_sz>0`, the check is false and execution reaches `memcpy(out, in, n)` and `out[n] = '\0'` with `out` NULL.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `copy_name(in="A", out=NULL, out_sz=2)`; `n=strnlen(in,1)` then `memcpy` dereferences NULL.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The guard `if (out || out_sz == 0) return -1;` is inverted, so a NULL `out` can slip through and cause NULL-pointer dereference in `memcpy`/write.

results.raw_output: {"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-476","file":"safe_copy.c","line_start":3,"line_end":6,"vuln_claim":"The guard `if (out || out_sz == 0) return -1;` is inverted, so a NULL `out` can slip through and cause NULL-pointer dereference in `memcpy`/write.","proof":"When `out==NULL` and `out_sz>0`, the check is false and execution reaches `memcpy(out, in, n)` and `out[n] = '\\0'` with `out` NULL.","repro_steps":"Call `copy_name(in=\"A\", out=NULL, out_sz=2)`; `n=strnlen(in,1)` then `memcpy` dereferences NULL.","impact":"Crash (denial of service) and potential fault handling bypass if this function is reachable with attacker-controlled pointers.","confidence":0.9,"chain_steps":"User supplies `out=NULL` with `out_sz>0` -> guard fails -> `memcpy`/`out[n]` dereference NULL -> process fault."}]}
